subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Completed some college coursework in Information Technology but did not obtain a degree
Participated in several online certifications related to customer service and technical support (e.g., CompTIA IT Fundamentals, Google IT Support Certificate)
Attended company-sponsored workshops on software troubleshooting and client communication
Received on-the-job training for software tools used by the support team
Engaged in peer-led learning sessions within the organization to stay updated on new technologies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Holds the title of Customer Support Manager at a software company
Responsible for supervising and leading a team of support specialists
Oversees the resolution of customer complaints and technical issues via multiple channels
Acts as a liaison between support staff and upper management, conveying feedback and service improvements
Manages workflow, escalates complex issues, and ensures SLA compliance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the technology sector, specifically in software services
Supports clients using SaaS products developed by the company
Operates within the broader IT and customer experience industry
Interfaces with both technical and non-technical users in the software domain

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a mid-sized software company with approximately 200-500 employees
Works in an open, collaborative office environment with opportunities for remote work
Company culture emphasizes teamwork, continuous improvement, and customer-centricity
The support team consists of around 10-15 people, with frequent cross-departmental collaboration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Company provides B2B software solutions for organizational workflow management
Primary clients are other businesses needing robust support for their operations
Products cater to industries such as finance, healthcare, and education
Focuses on delivering scalable, cloud-based tools for business process automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has held the Customer Support Manager position for 3-5 years
Previously worked as a frontline support specialist for 2 years prior to promotion
Total experience in the software support domain is approximately 5-7 years
Stepped into a supervisory role after demonstrating strong leadership and technical skills

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Occupies a mid-level management role overseeing a team of specialists
Has direct reports and is responsible for their performance reviews
Participates in departmental planning and process optimization
Acts as a point of escalation for complex or sensitive customer issues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Supervises a customer support team that handles incoming tickets via Intercom, ServiceNow, and Zendesk
Troubleshoots complex client issues and ensures timely resolution
Monitors ticket queues and redistributes workload as necessary
Provides coaching and feedback to support agents
Prepares weekly reports on support metrics for upper management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends roughly 40% of work time overseeing and responding to customer tickets
Devotes 20% to team management and staff development activities
Allocates 15% to process improvement and documentation
Spends 15% on administrative duties and tool management (e.g., ActiveAdmin)
Dedicates 10% to cross-team collaboration and meetings

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has significant autonomy in managing the daily operations of the support team
Empowered to make decisions regarding ticket prioritization and escalation
Can implement process improvements and propose tool changes within the team
Consults with senior management for high-impact or strategic decisions
Has authority to approve time-off and schedule changes for direct reports

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Quickly assesses and triages customer tickets based on urgency and complexity
Communicating with clients to clarify and resolve straightforward issues
Navigating support platforms like Intercom and ServiceNow with confidence
Coaching team members on standard troubleshooting procedures

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Resolving multifaceted technical issues that span multiple systems
Handling escalated complaints from high-value or dissatisfied customers
Integrating new tools or updating workflows across the team
Managing conflicts or performance issues among support staff

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Manually categorizing and tagging incoming tickets for routing
Compiling weekly and monthly support metrics for reporting
Updating knowledge base articles with minor revisions
Processing standard password reset or account unlock requests

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys coaching team members and seeing their skills improve over time
Finds satisfaction in resolving complicated customer issues
Prefers brainstorming sessions for process improvements
Finds compiling repetitive reports or updating documentation tedious

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Duplicate ticket creation due to customers using multiple channels
Poor integration between ServiceNow and Intercom leading to manual data entry
Difficulty tracking ticket status when agents forget to update records
Occasional delays in accessing up-to-date knowledge base resources

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Higher enjoyment typically correlates with greater productivity and quality
Tasks that match personal strengths, like troubleshooting, are completed more efficiently
Tedious or repetitive tasks lead to lower engagement and increased risk of errors
Continuous learning and mastering new skills contribute to job satisfaction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Has a generally positive attitude towards adopting new technologies
Sees value in tools that improve team efficiency or customer satisfaction
Sometimes feels overwhelmed by the pace of change in the tech landscape
Prefers hands-on, practical training over theoretical instruction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to incremental changes in existing platforms
Requires more time to adjust to major overhauls or entirely new tools
Relies on early access or sandbox environments to experiment before full rollout
Values clear documentation and peer support during transitions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours per month to structured tech training
Prefers short, focused training modules over lengthy courses
More likely to engage in training when it is directly relevant to current responsibilities
Occasionally participates in vendor-led webinars and in-house workshops

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential for improved team performance and reduced workload
Workload spikes can limit availability for voluntary training
Skeptical of tools that appear to add complexity without clear benefit
Encouraged by management’s support and recognition for upskilling

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers who embrace new tools positively influence adoption rates across the team
Management endorsement and resource allocation are key drivers for tool uptake
Often pilots new software with a small group before wider rollout
Openly shares feedback with both peers and supervisors about technology changes

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses Intercom as the primary chat system for customer inquiries
Relies on ServiceNow and Zendesk for ticket management and escalation
Administers user roles and permissions through ActiveAdmin
Collaborates with the team via Slack for internal communication
Leverages Asana for task and project tracking

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Regularly uses Lessonly for team onboarding and training
Accesses Seismic Knowledge for updated product documentation
Utilizes Google Workspace (Docs, Sheets, Drive) for documentation and reporting
Employs screen-sharing tools such as Zoom to assist customers directly

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Skilled in using major helpdesk platforms like Zendesk and ServiceNow
Comfortable with project management systems such as Asana and Trello
Experienced with chat and collaboration tools including Slack and Microsoft Teams
Understands basic CRM functions but does not use Salesforce extensively

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Faced challenges syncing data between ServiceNow and Intercom, resulting in duplicate records
Occasional delays when transferring customer information between platforms
Manual workarounds are sometimes needed due to incomplete tool integrations
Has advocated for improved API connections and workflow automations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts like machine learning, natural language processing, and chatbots
Recognizes AI’s capabilities in automating routine support tasks
Occasionally reads articles or attends webinars on AI trends in customer support
Can explain terms such as 'training data', 'model output', and 'confidence score' at a high level

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Has used LLM-based chatbots integrated within Intercom to suggest responses
Experimented with AI-driven ticket classification features in ServiceNow
Tested AI content suggestions in Seismic Knowledge for knowledge base updates
Occasionally uses ChatGPT or similar tools for quick research or drafting replies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses AI features occasionally, primarily for drafting initial customer responses
Relies on AI-based ticket routing during high-volume periods
Employs AI to auto-suggest knowledge base articles for support agents
Less frequent use for analytics or reporting tasks, which are mostly manual

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI suggested a highly relevant solution for a recurring technical issue, reducing resolution time
Encountered an AI misclassification that routed a critical ticket to the wrong team
Learned that human review is essential before acting on AI-generated responses
Observed improved consistency in knowledge base updates when using AI-generated content as a starting point

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Received basic training during the rollout of AI features in Intercom
Peers often share tips and best practices for getting the most out of AI tools
No formal AI certification program in place within the company
Relies on vendor documentation and internal Slack channels for troubleshooting AI-related issues

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI, especially for complex or unfamiliar tasks
Appreciates the option to review and edit AI-generated suggestions before sending to customers
Finds independent use more efficient for simple, repetitive queries
Wants the flexibility to toggle between modes depending on the situation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant to augment daily workflow
Occasionally uses AI as an advisor for troubleshooting rare technical issues
Does not rely on AI as a full co-worker; prefers to maintain final say on customer communications
Sees value in AI supporting but not replacing human judgment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to experimenting with AI-driven processes that clearly demonstrate efficiency gains
Skeptical of workflows that reduce transparency or hinder quality control
Willingness increases when peers or management actively support adoption
Prefers gradual integration over abrupt transitions to AI-heavy processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Finds conversational interfaces more intuitive for customer interaction scenarios
Uses command-based tools for technical administration tasks in ActiveAdmin
Appreciates hybrid interfaces that offer both conversational guidance and command shortcuts
Believes conversational UI helps new team members ramp up faster

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many customer issues require context-specific product knowledge not easily encoded in documentation
Relies on experience to interpret nuanced customer feedback and tone
AI systems sometimes miss subtle cues or history relevant to a given client
Tacit knowledge is especially important for high-stakes or escalated cases

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for low-risk, repetitive tasks like ticket categorization
Exercises caution and double-checks AI outputs for complex or sensitive responses
Has experienced both accurate and flawed AI recommendations, reinforcing the need for oversight
Would not rely solely on AI for decisions impacting customer satisfaction or retention

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle initial triage and suggest solutions, with humans refining and approving outputs
Believes repetitive tasks should be highly automated to free up staff for complex problem-solving
Wants final review of customer-facing communications to remain human-led
Open to increasing automation as AI systems improve reliability

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for simple, low-stakes tasks with clear outcomes
Feels automation is threatening if it reduces transparency or introduces errors without human checks
Wants clear escalation paths when automated processes fail
More comfortable with automation when override options are built-in

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of critical thinking and interpersonal skills
Routine troubleshooting skills may become less crucial as automation improves
Sees ongoing need for deep product knowledge and customer empathy
Views adaptability and tech literacy as increasingly important in the evolving landscape

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing skills in AI prompt engineering for effective use of LLMs
Taking responsibility for reviewing and refining AI-generated content
Participates in evaluating AI tool performance and suggesting improvements
Guides team members on best practices for integrating AI into support workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Has some concerns about automation reducing the need for basic support roles
Believes managerial and customer relationship skills are less likely to be replaced by AI
Watches industry trends but feels secure given the emphasis on team leadership
Encourages team members to upskill in areas that complement automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: AI-generated responses in Intercom require agent review before being sent
Manual ticket reassignment is possible if AI misroutes a case
Knowledge base updates generated by AI are subject to human approval
Has advocated for more transparent override options in tool feature requests

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company has gradually increased adoption of AI features over the past 2 years
Policy emphasizes human oversight and accountability in all AI-assisted processes
Encourages continuous feedback from staff on AI tool performance
No significant layoffs or role reductions attributed to AI adoption so far

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will continue to transform customer service and support industries
Sees potential for AI to create new job categories even as it automates others
Worries about possible bias or loss of human touch in customer interactions
Expects regulatory frameworks to shape AI adoption in coming years

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Values transparency in how AI decisions are made and communicated to staff
Concerned about data privacy and security when using AI tools
Believes AI should augment, not replace, human judgment in sensitive cases
Supports clear guidelines and accountability for AI-driven decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from more accurate AI-driven ticket prioritization based on sentiment analysis
Desires AI summarization tools to quickly brief staff on ongoing customer cases
Wants improved integrations between AI features across all support platforms
Sees value in AI-powered real-time coaching for support agents during live chats

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects the support manager role to focus increasingly on overseeing AI-human collaboration
Predicts team size may shrink as automation handles more routine inquiries
Foresees greater emphasis on customer relationship management and complex escalations
Anticipates needing to continuously learn and adapt to new AI-driven processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Invest in regular training to keep staff up to date with AI advancements
Establish clear protocols for human oversight of AI outputs
Solicit ongoing feedback from frontline staff on AI tool performance
Prioritize integration and data interoperability between AI and existing systems
